questions.q: Name the three types of front-end code library identified in the syllabus dot-point.
options: Frameworks, template engines, and predesigned CSS classes
Libraries, frameworks, and APIs
React, Bootstrap, and Jinja
Component libraries, package managers, and build tools
questions.explanation: The dot-point names exactly three types: frameworks, template engines, and predesigned CSS classes. The mnemonic is F-T-C, 'for the code' — Frameworks for complex apps, Template engines for server-side rendering, CSS classes for ready styling. React, Bootstrap and Jinja (option C) are specific examples of these types, not the type names themselves, so naming them would not earn the recall mark.

questions.q: Which statement correctly captures the verbatim one-liner the episode says to memorise about libraries and frameworks?
options: You call a library; a framework calls you
A framework is larger and more popular than a library
A library handles styling while a framework handles logic
A framework runs on the server; a library runs in the browser
questions.explanation: The episode says to memorise it verbatim: 'You call a library; a framework calls you.' With a library your own code is in control and reaches in for a function, whereas a framework provides the structure and calls your code at points it defines. The distinction is not about size or popularity (option B) — that is the very confusion the episode warns against.

questions.q: Distinguish between a library and a framework using the concept the episode says earns the discriminating mark.
options: A library is open-source whereas a framework is commercial
A library is written in JavaScript whereas a framework can use any language
A library is called by the developer's code and leaves architectural control with the developer, whereas a framework imposes structure and calls the developer's code — an inversion of control
A library provides styling whereas a framework provides routing and state management
questions.explanation: The discriminating mark is naming inversion of control — who controls the flow. With a library the developer's own code is the boss and calls in; with a framework the framework is the boss and calls your code at defined points (the 'Hollywood principle'). Option D lists real framework features but misses the underlying distinction that the exam rewards, which is about who is in charge of the flow.

questions.q: Outline what a template engine such as Jinja does and explain why this topic 'spills across the Front-Back line'.
options: It bundles JavaScript in the browser to reduce download size, so it lives purely on the Front
It inserts data into an HTML template on the server to generate finished HTML before sending the page — server-side rendering, which lives on the Back
It applies ready-made CSS classes to style components on the client side
It manages application state and routing for single-page applications
questions.explanation: A template engine lets the server insert real data — a user's name, a list of products — into an HTML template with placeholders and loops, producing finished HTML before sending it. This is server-side rendering, which is why the topic spills onto the Back (server side) even though front-end libraries mostly live on the Front. Option D describes a framework's job (state and routing), not a template engine's.

questions.q: What capabilities does a front-end framework like React, Vue, or Angular provide that make it suited to complex applications?
options: Server-side rendering, database access, and email handling
Image compression, caching, and offline storage
Predesigned buttons, cards, and forms you drop in
Component architecture, state management, and routing
questions.explanation: A front-end framework gives you the machinery a big interactive app needs: a component architecture to build the UI from reusable pieces, state management to keep data consistent across the app, and routing to navigate between views. This is what you reach for in a single-page application (SPA) where the page never fully reloads. Option C describes predesigned CSS classes (the 'C' in F-T-C), not a framework.

questions.q: According to the BUILD-versus-BUY rule, when should a developer BUY (adopt an existing framework or library)?
options: When needs are unique, full control is required, and maximum performance matters
Whenever a modern framework is available, because a framework is always better
Only when the page is mostly static with very little interactive state
When needs are standard, the timeline is tight, and the team already knows the tool
questions.explanation: BUY when needs are standard, the timeline is tight, and the team knows the tool — you gain speed, tested and reliable code, community support, and consistency. Option A is the BUILD case (unique needs, full control, peak performance). Option B is one of the two named traps — 'a framework is always better' — which the episode explicitly says is wrong because it leads to over-engineering.

questions.q: Compare predesigned CSS classes (e.g. Bootstrap, Tailwind) with writing CSS from scratch, linking back to the previous episode's principles.
options: Predesigned classes run on the server, so they offer better security than hand-written CSS
Predesigned classes give consistency and maintainability off the shelf, applying the design-token and reuse benefits except someone else already built the system
Hand-written CSS is always faster to load because it never adds any bundle weight
Predesigned classes replace JavaScript, removing the need for any interactive behaviour
questions.explanation: Predesigned CSS classes are pre-built, pre-styled UI pieces you drop in for instant, professional-looking buttons, cards, and forms — this is the consistency and maintainability of last episode's design tokens and reuse, except someone else already built the system. The trade-off the episode notes is that you pull in the framework's stylesheet, adding bundle size. Option A is wrong because CSS classes are applied on the client, not the server.

questions.q: A team needs a simple marketing landing page — mostly static, a little styling, and one contact form. A junior says 'use React because React is modern and powerful.' What is the strong, mark-earning recommendation?
options: Use React, because it provides routing and state management that any modern site needs
Use a different framework such as Vue, because it has a smaller learning curve than React
Build it with plain HTML, CSS, and a little JavaScript, because a full framework adds unnecessary complexity and a large bundle for a page with almost no interactive state
Use a template engine like Jinja, because server-side rendering makes static pages load faster
questions.explanation: For a simple, mostly static page the strong answer is BUILD with vanilla HTML, CSS, and minimal JavaScript — adopting a heavy framework is over-engineering that adds a large bundle to download for a page with little interactive state. Reaching reflexively for React (option A) is exactly the trap the episode names; matching tool weight to problem complexity means simple-and-bespoke equals build. Swapping to a different framework (option B) still over-engineers the page.

questions.q: A team is building a live dashboard single-page application with many interactive components and shared state. Recommend an approach and justify it.
options: Adopt a framework like React or Vue, because its component architecture, state management, and routing handle this complexity, speeding development and improving maintainability and consistency
Build it from scratch in vanilla JavaScript for full control over every component
Use predesigned CSS classes such as Bootstrap, because consistent styling is the main requirement of a dashboard
Use a template engine for server-side rendering, because dashboards never need client-side interactivity
questions.explanation: This is the complex-and-standard case, so the strong answer is BUY: adopt a framework whose component architecture, state management, and routing handle exactly this complexity, dramatically speeding development and improving maintainability and consistency. Building all that machinery bespoke in vanilla JavaScript (option B) would be slow and error-prone. Predesigned CSS classes (option C) only solve styling, not the shared interactive state that defines this app.

questions.q: An 'assess' question asks you to weigh up adopting a front-end framework. Beyond the speed and consistency gained, which cost does the episode say students most often forget?
options: That frameworks require a paid licence to use in production
That frameworks cannot be combined with predesigned CSS classes
That frameworks add bundle size — more JavaScript for the browser to download and run — which slows the page, especially on mobile and slow connections
That frameworks prevent the use of a template engine for server-side rendering
questions.explanation: The cost students forget is the price of adoption: a framework adds bundle size, meaning more JavaScript for the browser to download and execute, which slows the page especially on mobile and slow connections — a direct forward link to episode 13-3 on performance and page-load management. An 'assess' answer needs both sides: the speed and consistency gained against the bundle size, learning curve, and lock-in paid. Licensing cost (option A) is not the bundle-size point the episode stresses here.
